data.title: The Market's Big Problem Is Persistence
data.description:
By Geoff Bysshe

Where Persistence Has Become A Big Problem

If you’ve been following this article over the last several weeks, you may have noticed a theme that focuses on the importance of time as an influence on market behavior.

  • The market's time in a trend period has an effect.
  • Investors' patience with the trend period has an effect.
  • Investors’ reactions to time are often gradual, then excessive.

Time in a trend affects sentiment, positioning, and more.

The fundamental factors behind the market’s trend are also likely to be affected over time, as is the case with the persistence of the Iran war and the elevated price of oil, both together and independently.

Few would argue that the higher price of oil, driven by the war, has depressed stock prices and expectations for global economic growth, but how quickly the market and economies will recover should the war come to an end in the next week, month, quarter, or longer is debatable.

However, the longer the war goes on, keeping oil prices elevated, oil production declining, and the flow of oil to importing countries curtailed, the bigger the problem becomes.

Higher oil prices are an obvious problem, so when the war ends, the next obvious question will be, "When and for how long will oil prices fall?"

There’s never one reason for a price trend, but below you’ll find a visual analysis of the relationship between the changes and levels of OECD Oil Inventory Data and the price of crude oil. Note that this data excludes strategic reserves, in order to put more focus on the open market source of oil.

As you can see, there are three measures.

The top chart shows the level of commercial stocks (inventory excluding strategic reserves) and the price of oil. It’s clear that the price of oil is inversely correlated with the level and trend of inventories.

The middle chart shows month-to-month changes in inventory along with a three-month average of the monthly changes and the price of oil. Again, the price of oil is highly correlated with the 3-month average trend and level of inventory changes.

The bottom row has two charts that give the correlation data for the one-month period and the total level. Here you can see clearly that one-month changes aren’t a problem; however, the cumulative impact over time is highly correlated.

A persistent trend of declining inventories is a problem that leads to persistently higher oil prices.

In case you didn’t notice, the reporting of data lags by a couple of months. So, while this is the most recent data, it doesn’t include the impact of the war.

How Will This Market Bottom?

There are three common ways market corrections and bear markets bottom out.

  1. The first is quick and violent capitulation;
  2. The second is a process of sector rotation; and
  3. The third is through a long depression of investor sentiment, combined with compressed price action.

The investors and the media are looking out for capitulation (number 1), but the market is saying it prefers rotation right now.

In other words, while the market is trading like a bear market and the media’s reports often sound dire, the market is not selling everything in a rush of capitulation.

If you’re only looking at the S&P 500 or NASDAQ 100, it may feel like capitulation is underway, but the broader market tells a different story.

As a result, the process of sector rotation can indicate when the market has bottomed and/or when the market slides into a state of capitulation.

When Will This Market Bottom?

In last week’s Market Outlook, we introduced the PRIME framework for analyzing a market’s trend to determine whether it is likely to continue or reverse.

As with most good frameworks or methodologies, it has basic principles you can use immediately to improve your market analysis. Additionally, with experience, you’ll discover several levels of detail that make the PRIME method even more powerful.

The basics look like this.

The PRIME framework is based on 5 primary conditions that influence a stock’s or market’s trend. They are as follows:

  1. Price trends and patterns: Market moves create and enhance emotions like fear, greed, confidence, hope, etc.
  2. Relative performance: The best- and worst-performing stocks attract investors’ attention.
  3. Institutional buying and selling: The largest, most durable trends are driven by institutions.
  4. Momentum: Prices and investor emotions have a measurable momentum factor that contributes to the strength of a trend.
  5. Expected Earnings Growth: The foundational reason for owning a stock is based on a belief that the company will grow its earnings and value.

The PRIME method provides a simple way to measure all five elements:

  1. Price Trend & Patterns: Market Phases indicators
  2. Relative Performance: Triple Play Leadership indicators
  3. Institutional Accumulation: Triple Play Volume Trend indicators
  4. Momentum: Real Motion indicators
  5. Expected Earnings or A Growth Story: This is more subjective.

This market will have established a reliable bottom when three of the four top PRIME criteria are bullish in the indexes, and/or when several growth-oriented sectors are also in bullish conditions based on the PRIME criteria.

Here are a few sector charts explained.

XLE, the energy ETF, is a good example of what a clear breakout looks like with all the PRIME indicators in a bullish condition. The charts that follow are not very bullish, but they are showing signs of holding or turning in a market where the indexes (SPY and QQQ) are very bearish.

XLP, staples, is a defensive sector, but it’s holding its 200 DMA while the market trades much lower than its 200 MA. Real Motion moving averages often identify support areas, so the Real Motion chart on the 200 MA is significant.

XLB, materials, has moved over its 10 DMA, and the Real Motion 10/50 momentum indicator’s green dots confirm there may be enough momentum to continue higher.

Additionally, the leadership indicator has a bullish reading, which suggests the sector’s improving price action is not a result of following the market. This conclusion is easy and obvious in the current bearish market environment, but when the market is trending higher, a leadership indicator with this pattern highlights the strongest trends.

XLU, utilities, broke down below the important support shown by the dotted line. However, it held its 50 DMA and maintained its strong leadership condition. If XLU trades over its 10 DMA and the resistance of the breakdown level, Real Motion is in a position to turn bullish, which would create a very bullish chart.

Stocks That Can Do Well Now and When The War Ends

One way to apply the PRIME method is to focus on stocks in leading trends with a compelling growth story.

The AI-driven trend of data center construction has created enormous growth prospects for the fiberoptics industry. Three companies that are well-positioned to experience strong growth for the foreseeable future in this industry are GLW (Corning, Inc.), CIEN (Ciena Corp.) and AAOI (Applied Optoelectronics, Inc.).

Despite the war and weak market, these stocks have had large moves higher over the last month. As you’ll see in their charts below, they all pushed higher with “all bullish” PRIME conditions.

Timing trades is based on price patterns and trading strategies. The PRIME indicators highlight when the conditions for a strong trend or reversals exist.

In the last week, these stocks have begun to pull back, and some of the PRIME conditions have turned bearish.

This creates opportunities to buy a correction in a strong long-term trend when the intermediate- and/or longer-term trend resumes its upmove as defined by the PRIME indicators.

Below you’ll find a quick analysis similar to what we did on the sector charts above.

Corning, Inc.

GLW has a strong growth story.

  • Strong revenue acceleration in 2025 - FY2025 revenue surged 19% to $15.6B, the best growth in years, driven by the Optical Communications segment benefiting from AI data center buildout. Estimates call for FY2026 revenue of $18.8B (+20%) and FY2027 of $21.4B (+14%).
  • EPS poised for a major step-up - Consensus FY2026 EPS of $5.09 represents a 174% increase over FY2025 GAAP EPS of $1.86, reflecting both operating leverage and absence of one-time charges. The 5-year CAGR (FY2020–FY2025) stands at 28.0% on EPS.
  • Reasonable valuation given growth trajectory - Forward P/E of 28.7x on FY2026e EPS and P/S of 8.0x TTM revenue are moderate for a company with 20%+ revenue growth and expanding margins. The trailing P/E of 80x reflects depressed FY2024/2025 GAAP earnings.
  • Consistent quarterly EPS beats - All four recent quarters showed adjusted EPS beats vs. consensus, with revenue generally in-line. Q3 2025 GAAP EPS was impacted by a non-cash impairment charge (negative GAAP EPS), but adjusted EPS remained positive.

Its technicals have deteriorated, but they could recover quickly.

The weakness in the market over the last two days has pulled GLW back under the 10 DMA (magenta) and down to the 50 DMA.

Warning: Both Real Motion indicators have red dots (bearish) under their 50 DMA, while the price chart sits over its 50 DMA. This condition suggests that if the price breaks its 50 DMA, it will continue lower.

However, if price moves back over its 10 DMA and the Real Motion charts turn green, everything will be in gear for a new move higher.

Applied Optoelectronics, Inc.

  • Explosive revenue growth in 2025 - Full-year 2025 revenue surged 83% to $455.7M from $249.4M in 2024, driven by strong data center demand for optical interconnects. Consensus estimates project ~$962M in 2026 (+111%) and ~$1.88B in 2027 (+95%).
  • Company approaching profitability - While all historical EPS figures are negative, AAOI posted only -$0.04 EPS in Q4 2025 and analysts estimate positive net income beginning in Q2 2026 (~$0.06/share). Full-year 2026 consensus EPS estimate is ~$0.95.
  • Elevated P/S valuation reflects high growth expectations - At a price/sales ratio of 18.9x on TTM revenue, the stock is priced for continued hyper-growth. P/E, PEG, and forward multiples are not meaningful given recent losses.

AAOI has a very similar situation as GLW above, but its momentum is stronger, and it’s further from its 50 DMA.

If it climbs back over its 10 DMA and the $100 level that has been pivotal, the price action will suggest it’s ready to move higher. If that happens with bullish reading in leadership, volume, and Real Motion, it will have all the ingredients of an explosive growth stock.

Ciena Inc.

CIEN’s growth story is strong, but it’s led by its sales growth:

  • Explosive EPS recovery expected in FY2026 - Consensus estimates call for EPS of $7.48 in FY2026 vs. $0.87 in FY2025, a ~760% surge. This reflects anticipated acceleration from AI-driven data center buildouts and strong demand for coherent optical networking infrastructure.
  • Revenue inflection in progress - 5yr Sales CAGR of 6.2% understates recent momentum. FY2025 revenue of $4.77B was up 19% YoY, and TTM revenue reached $5.12B. Consensus projects $6.1B in FY2026 (+28%) and $7.35B in FY2027 (+20%).
  • Consistent EPS misses, strong revenue beats - In each of the last 4 quarters, actual EPS came in below consensus estimates. Revenue, however, beat expectations in all 4 quarters, suggesting strong top line execution but elevated cost structures weighing on GAAP profitability.
  • Rich valuation discounts near-term earnings, prices in a recovery - At a P/E of 275x on trailing EPS of $1.59, the stock price of $437.70 is almost entirely forward-looking. The forward P/E of 58.5x (on FY2026 est. EPS of $7.48) and P/S of 12.1x reflect high investor confidence in the AI capex tailwind.

Its technical setup is also very strong

CIEN has had a very consistently strong 2026, as seen by its leadership and volume trend indicators, having a solid trend, and the red line remaining well above the blue line.

The recent pullback under the 10 DMA has landed right on a good level to bottom - the support of the $360 level, which was its February swing high.

Leadership and volume trend remain bullish, and while the Real Motion momentum measures have turned negative, they are at levels that are likely to be support. If CIEN gets back over its 10 DMA and $400, it’s likely that everything will be lined up for a continued move higher.

As the market corrects, you can see if these three stocks and other market leaders are ready to resume their uptrend by looking at how their PRIME trends hold up or deteriorate.

Best wishes for your trading.

Big View Summary

By Keith Schneider

Every week, we review the big picture of the market's technical condition as seen through the lens of our Big View data charts.

The bullets provide a quick summary organized by conditions we see as being risk-on, risk-off, or neutral.

Summary: Despite markets making new lows and broad risk-off signals dominating - rising volatility, weak breadth, sector rotation into defensives, and higher rates - there are modest signs of stabilization with improving internals, resilient “Modern Family” components, and relative strength outside the U.S. Overall, conditions remain pressured and oversold with a bearish tilt, but divergences and seasonal tailwinds suggest the potential for a near-term bounce rather than continued straight-line downside.

Neutral

  • With the market making new lows, the market internals are giving a weak divergent reading with up-down volume and advance-decline improving week over week and the McClellan Oscillator holding last week's levels. (=)
  • The risk gauge is showing neutral with the relative strength in rates and weakness in metals. (=)
  • The Modern Family held up relatively well this week, holding last week's lows across the board while the broader market broke down. Many are still outperforming the S&P and oversold on Real Motion. (=)
  • Emerging and developed markets are trending lower but put in more of an inside week and held up better than the US markets. (=)
  • Seasonally, we are past the typical February and March weakness, and April tends to pick up. (=)

Risk-Off

  • Volume patterns lean heavily towards distribution in all except IWM, which is more neutral. (-)
  • Three of the four indexes made new recent lows on the move this week. They are each below their 200-Day Moving Average and oversold on real motion. (-)
  • Sectors skewed risk-off with Technology and Financials hit the worst, while risk-off sectors like Utilities and consumer staples were up. (-)
  • New high new low closed slightly above last week’s levels, though still a negative reading overall. (-)
  • The color charts (moving average of stocks above key moving averages) continue to trend lower. (-)
  • Volatility made a new high this week, the highest reading going back to April 2025. It is overbought, though not at extreme levels. (-)
  • Growth plummeted this week even as value maintained its relative value and is still holding its 200-Day Moving Average. (-)
  • Soft commodities push higher, signalling inflationary pressure. (-)
  • Oil closed at a new high with continued concern over Middle East conflict. (-)
  • Rates continued to trend higher as the Fed is handcuffed and markets are expecting higher rates. (-)

Actionable Trading Plan

Core Positioning (Bias: Risk-Off but Oversold Bounce Potential)

  • Maintain a defensive posture overall, but be prepared to tactically deploy into a short-term bounce rather than pressing shorts at current extremes.
  • Favor value over growth and defensive sectors (utilities, staples), while growth remains weak and below key trend structures.

What to Do Now

  • Reduce or avoid new short exposure unless markets re-accelerate lower - conditions are already stretched with volatility elevated.
  • Look to scale into strength (not weakness) in areas showing relative resilience: select “Modern Family” leaders and non-U.S. equities that held up on the breakdown.
  • Keep position sizes smaller than normal given elevated volatility and unstable breadth.

Bounce Strategy (Primary Opportunity)

  • Expect a reflex rally driven by oversold conditions + seasonal tailwind (April).
  • On a bounce, sell into strength in weak sectors (tech, financials) rather than chasing upside.
  • Use the bounce to rebalance toward stronger themes (defensive/value, commodities with inflation tailwinds).

Risk Management

  • Respect that rates + oil + inflation pressures = headwind for equities, so avoid overcommitting to risk.
  • If volatility continues to expand or breadth fails to confirm a bounce, tighten exposure quickly and raise cash.
  • Stay flexible - this is a trading environment, not a trend-following one right now.

Tactical Tilt

  • Slight overweight: defensives, value, select commodities
  • Underweight: growth, high beta, rate-sensitive equities

Bottom Line

Play for a short-term bounce within a broader risk-off regime, using strength to de-risk and reposition rather than assuming a durable trend reversal.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SCHG: Strong Companies At An Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Investment And Thesis

The Schwab U.S. Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (ETF) that aims to provide exposure to the growth segment of U.S. large-cap equities. This exposure is achieved by tracking the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index with meaningful concentration across technology and communication services.

SCHG is down 13% year-to-date and 16.7% from its all-time high, primarily due to multiple compression. This is due to rising macroeconomic uncertainty stemming from geopolitical tensions as well as tariff policy uncertainties. That pressure has created an attractive entry point for SCHG, in my opinion. The fund’s top holdings all have strong revenue growth, expanding profit margins, and expected strong forward earnings growth. As a result, I rate SCHG a Buy.

Fund Overview

SCHG tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index, which is produced by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC and licensed to Schwab. The index is constructed via a screening process.

First, the top 750 U.S. publicly traded companies by market capitalization are ranked in order. This ensures the fund solely focuses on large-cap with no small- or mid-cap exposure.

Second, each security is scored on six factors with an equal mix of historical performance, current earnings, and expected future growth. These factors are projected price-to-earnings ratio (P/E), projected earnings per share (EPS) growth, price-to-book ratio (P/B), dividend yield, trailing twelve-month (TTM) revenue growth, and TTM EPS growth. Finally, the companies that exhibit promising relative growth are included in the fund and weighted by market capitalization.

In addition, SCHG rebalances on a quarterly basis. Its quarterly rebalance is simply adjusting the weight of the portfolio, which ensures no stock exceeds 24% of the fund and that stocks that are above 4.5% weight collectively cannot exceed 48% of the fund. This ensures the fund does not solely focus on a few stocks that experience a surge in price, such as Nvidia (NVDA).

SCHG charges an expense ratio of 0.04%, which is lower than most funds due to its passive tracking nature and securities lending, which assists in lowering costs. This ETF is also very liquid, with an average daily volume of 21 million shares.

SCHG also distributes a small dividend, with a TTM dividend yield of 0.45%. This negligible dividend is by design, as SCHG’s top holdings have historically returned capital to shareholders via buybacks rather than dividends. In addition, as this fund focuses on growth-oriented equities, these companies rarely distribute large dividends as they focus on their underlying business.

SCHG is also relatively tax efficient, as portfolio turnover is on the lower end at 19%. As a passively managed fund, SCHG is not frequently selling positions and realizing capital gains that would otherwise be passed through to shareholders. In addition, when SCHG rebalances, it is typically adjusting weights rather than replacing holdings, further limiting taxable events. This assists in tax efficiency to reduce the drag of annual capital gains distributions.

Portfolio Holdings & Valuation

As of March 28, 2026, SCHG holds 196 individual equities, with their top 10 holdings accounting for 57.36% of total assets. Looking at their top 5 holdings, we can see that Nvidia, Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), and Alphabet (GOOG) make up 43.19% of the fund. While these companies have delivered consistent significant growth and expect continued growth, they have experienced steep drawdowns as part of a broader market selloff, primarily driven by tariff uncertainty and geopolitical tensions.

I compiled the table above to present forward EPS estimates sourced from Seeking Alpha and calculated the respective forward P/E, estimated forward EPS growth, and forward PEG ratios for each of the top holdings.

While all of their holdings are expected to deliver significant growth in the next twelve months, we can see that NVDA stands out on a valuation basis. With a forward PE of 15x and a forward PEG of 0.12, we can see the market appears to be significantly underpricing its growth trajectory, despite consistent commentary from CEO Jensen Huang. Huang has recently spoken publicly about visibility to $1 trillion in revenue due to strong demand for its Blackwell and Vera Rubin Graphic Processing Units (GPU). As emphasized by the hyperscalers (MSFT, GOOG, and AMZN), supply is nowhere near demand.

Conversely, AAPL appears to carry the highest relative valuation with a forward PE of 26.7x and a forward PEG of 1.08. While their PEG carries a premium, AAPL is still expected to deliver strong earnings growth through its expanding services segment, a recovery in China phone volumes, and executing record share buybacks due to their strong free cash flow.

Finally, the three hyperscalers, trading at approximately 18-21x forward earnings, are expected to deliver strong earnings growth driven by re-acceleration in their respective cloud businesses and operating margin expansion as cloud pricing strengthens and capital expenditures begin to generate returns on investment.

Performance & Peer Comparison

I’ve compiled a table that compares SCHG to similar funds such as VUG and QQQ due to their overlapping exposure to technology stocks and similar investment philosophies.

Looking at total return, we can see that SCHG has delivered competitive returns, delivering a 3-year CAGR of 27.37%, a 5-year CAGR of 14.48%, and a 10-year CAGR of 18.32%. We can see that SCHG has outperformed VUG across every time horizon other than a year and has slightly underperformed QQQ on a 1, 3, and 5-year time horizon. In addition, we can see that SCHG has tracked their benchmark well, with only a few basis points lost to tracking error.

Risks

As of March 28, 2026, the current macroeconomic background is volatile with several near-term headwinds. President Donald Trump has maintained and escalated his position on tariffs, threatening supply chains. In addition, the U.S. continues its conflict with Iran, resulting in sustained elevated oil prices. This increase in energy costs further threatens supply chains while contributing to continued inflation, reducing the chance of rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. This environment is typically hostile for growth-oriented equities, which are more sensitive to higher discount rates.

According to CME FedWatch, the current market consensus is a 74.2% chance of no rate change, a 22.5% chance of one rate hike, and a 2% chance of two rate hikes. A higher rate environment is not favorable for high-beta names such as technology companies. For a fund like SCHG that focuses on growth-oriented equities, this presents a meaningful headwind for future growth.

Balance Sheet & FCF Risk

In addition, we can see that forward free cash flow in SCHG’s top holdings is expected to meaningfully contract, driven by accelerating AI infrastructure spend, particularly on NVDA's GPUs. I’ve compiled a table of the hyperscalers’ expected capital expenditures, TTM Operating Cash Flows, and the implied Free Cash Flow (FCF). As we can see, AMZN and GOOG are expected to produce negative FCF, with MSFT quickly approaching negative FCF. Investors who have historically relied on large-cap growth names to funnel high levels of FCF into share buybacks should temper their expectations as ambitious buildout plans take center stage.

As a result of this expected negative FCF, we can see that hyperscalers need additional capital to fund their capex. As a result, Amazon and Alphabet have raised debt to fund their ambitions. Amazon raised $54 billion in the U.S. and European bond markets, while Alphabet has tapped the bond market for nearly $32 billion. While both companies boast investment-grade credit ratings supported by their strong underlying business fundamentals, this is still a risk that needs to be monitored. Should the AI infrastructure buildout fail to generate meaningful returns on capital, the cost of carrying this debt can present a meaningful headwind.

Concentration Risk

SCHG is extremely concentrated, with the top 5 holdings making up 43% of the fund and the top 10 constituting 57%. Investors who purchase SCHG will be deeply exposed to AI and technology. In the event data centers fail to generate meaningful returns on capital, there is an outsized risk of steep drawdowns driven by investor pessimism. As an example, a 30% decline in NVDA alone would reduce SCHG’s NAV by roughly 3.5%. A 30% decline in AAPL would further reduce NAV by an additional 3.0%.

Who Should Buy SCHG?

SCHG is best suited for investors who are seeking growth-oriented companies, have a high-risk tolerance, and are bullish on the ongoing buildout of AI infrastructure. It is not suited for income-focused investors, as the fund’s TTM dividend yield of 0.45% is negligible. It is also not suitable for investors seeking international exposure, as the fund focuses on U.S. equities. SCHG is also a tax-efficient vehicle appropriate for many investment accounts, such as a taxable brokerage account, 401k, and IRA.

Conclusion

The fundamental bull cases for these companies remain durable. AI-driven capital expenditure by hyperscalers such as MSFT, AMZN, and GOOG remains strong, with all hyperscalers guiding for significant YoY growth in capex, a sign that AI demand continues to expand. NVDA continues to execute at the hardware and software layer, with strong visibility to $1 trillion in revenue. Every AI company continues to assert that the need for accelerated compute and inference infrastructure remains supply constrained and demand continues to be relentless. With SCHG trading approximately 16.7% from its ATH, forward P/Es on SCHG’s top holdings have compressed to an attractive level in my opinion. For investors with a long time horizon who are bullish on AI, I believe SCHG to be a Buy.

data.title: KBR: Low Valuation And Healthy Long-Term Drivers Make It A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

KBR (KBR) is trading at a significant discount versus historical levels. Its current forward P/E is over 40% lower than the 5-year historical average. While there are near-term headwinds due to the winding down of the Plaquemines LNG project in 2027 and geopolitical developments impacting Middle East projects, I believe these concerns are already getting priced in at current levels.

In the medium to long term, the company is well positioned to benefit from a strong focus on energy security globally and increasing defense spending. In the STS business, the company may get additional work to repair and rebuild war-related damages to energy infrastructure. Some of the delayed LNG projects, like the Lake Charles LNG project, may find financial backers due to the energy security theme. In MTS business, the company should benefit from increased defense logistics spending, and recent M&As like LinQuest should also help.

The stock is trading at a P/E in high single digits versus high teens historical valuations. Given good medium-term growth prospects and low valuations, I rate the stock a buy.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

I last covered KBR in June 2024, and it has not performed well since then. The company has seen TRANSCOM contract termination, and some of the LNG projects, like the Lake Charles Project, also got delayed. In Q4 2025, KBR’s revenue declined by 11% y/y to $1.9 bn. In the Mission Technology Solutions segment, revenue declined by 19% y/y due to a reduction in contingency EUCOM scope, NASA funding restrictions, new award delays, and procurement delays across US Government Defense and Intelligence clients. In the Sustainable Technology Solutions (STS) segment, revenues were down 2% y/y due to a decline in petrochemical capex and a pause in some green projects.

Looking ahead, while KBR faces some near-term headwinds, I remain optimistic about the company’s longer-term revenue prospects.

In the Sustainable Technology Solutions (STS) segment, recent backlog trends are encouraging. The company reported approximately 5% yr/yr growth in STS backlog, reaching around $4.2 billion at the end of fiscal 2025. Its book-to-bill was also a solid ~1.6x. Order trends and backlog are leading indicators of revenues, and this growth should support healthy revenue growth in 2026. The company’s strong competitive positioning in key technologies like LNG and ammonia should also benefit it further.

However, one key medium-term overhang is the anticipated completion of the Plaquemines LNG project. It is expected to wind down by early 2027. This creates a visibility gap, and investors are concerned about how the company will replace this revenue stream. Earlier expectations relied on the Lake Charles LNG project to ramp up and offset this decline. However, these hopes have been pushed out. The Lake Charles project has yet to secure final commercial backing. Energy Transfer (ET), the company executing this project, has shifted financial resources towards pipeline projects and is looking for third parties who may be interested in developing this project.

If we look at the stock price correction over the past few months, this Lake Charles project delay has been one of the main reasons behind its underperformance. However, global energy markets are shifting. Heightened geopolitical risks and supply disruptions may improve the economic rationale for large-scale LNG projects in the US. So, securing a third-party financial partner for Lake Charles looks more favorable than it did in prior periods. Should the project progress, it could help bridge the future revenue gap visibility.

The evolving geopolitical environment is also reinforcing the importance of energy security and independence. This remains a main driver for STS. While nearer-term disruptions from the Iran war may cause project delays or construction activity slowdown in the Middle East, the longer-term implications support capital investments in energy infrastructure, including new capacity additions as well as repairing facilities damaged by the current war. The company has a larger presence in the Middle East. There is no doubt that there are some short-term disruption risks due to war, but it also presents a long-term opportunity once stability returns, as the Middle East energy infrastructure remains crucial for global energy requirements.

Further, KBR is also shifting toward OpEx-oriented and recurring work, which provides an additional layer of support. Through organic initiatives and tuck-in acquisitions like SWAT, the company has been expanding its capabilities in areas such as asset utilization, digital solutions, and maintenance services within refinery, petrochemical, and renewable systems. This helps reduce KBR’s reliance on volatile, capital-intensive project cycles and should contribute to a much more stable revenue stream over time.

On the Mission Technology Solutions (MTS) side, revenue should see some headwinds in 1H26 as it laps tough comparisons and faces reduced EUCOM work. However, trends should improve as the year progresses. The company is well aligned with 2026 Defense Appropriations Act funding. Management anticipates bidding more than $25 billion in 2026, representing double-digit growth yr/yr. The company is also performing well internationally and has secured higher-value opportunities, including defense programs in Australia. Further, KBR’s positioning in digital engineering, space, and advanced defense capabilities is also strengthened by the recent LinQuest acquisition. This should support growth in higher-margin work in future years.

The evolving geopolitical situation, especially the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, could provide incremental growth for the MTS segment over the intermediate term. Usually, the periods of sustained military activity are followed by a heightened defense budget and rising demand for logistics, engineering, and support services.

Overall, while in the near term uncertainty persists around Lake Charles delays and Middle East project disruptions due to the Iran war, the broader demand backdrop remains positive. The structural tailwinds, such as energy security, a higher defense budget, and increasing demand for higher-value technology solutions, form a solid foundation for long-term growth. As these trends continue to play out, KBR looks well positioned to deliver good revenue growth in the long term.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

On margins, the company saw a 190 bps y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion in Q4 2025 despite revenue decline, benefiting from cost-saving initiatives, disciplined project execution, and favorable mix as low-margin EUCOM volumes declined. The MTS’ adjusted EBITDA margin was up 198 bps y/y. In the STS segment, the adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by 101 bps y/y.

I am optimistic about the company’s long-term margin prospects. The ongoing global rollout of the Microsoft Dynamics ERP system is expected to enhance project visibility, improve cost management, and drive better efficiency gains. At the same time, the company is expanding its recurring, OPEX-related business. This should contribute to more predictable and resilient margin prospects compared to traditional capex-driven project work.

On the MTS side, a shift away from margin-dilutive work, coupled with a focus on higher-value, technology-led contracts, should drive margin in coming years. The ongoing wind-down of margin-dilutive EUCOM-related activity should provide an uplift to profitability as the portfolio mix improves. The company is also positioning itself toward differentiated opportunities, encompassing digital engineering, space, and advanced defense solutions. The integration of LinQuest should further strengthen this position. LinQuest increases KBR’s exposure to higher-margin national security and space-related programs. Growth in global markets such as the U.K. and Australia adds incremental support to margin upside, as they carry much more favorable margins compared to domestic government work.

Valuation

KBR currently trades at ~9.22x FY26 consensus EPS of $4.01 and 8.65x FY27 consensus EPS of $4.28. This is a significant discount to its ﬁve-year average forward P/E multiple of around 17.68x. On an EV/EBITDA basis also, the stock trades at roughly 7.1x forward EV/EBITDA compared to a historical average closer to 11.72x.

There are two primary drivers behind this valuation discount. First is a revenue visibility gap within the STS segment with the anticipated wind-down of the Plaquemines LNG project by early 2027. Final commercial backing has yet to be secured by the Lake Charles LNG project, which could have helped the company replace lost revenues. Second is the near-term uncertainty around construction activity and execution timelines in key regions given the current geopolitical environment. These factors are weighing heavily on investor sentiment and recent stock performance.

However, the longer-term fundamentals of the business appear to be strengthening. Both STS and MTS should benefit from structural drivers such as the growing emphasis on energy independence, rising global demand for LNG, and sustained defense spending. In fact, the economic case for large-scale energy infrastructure investments like Lake Charles has been reinforced by the ongoing Iran war and closure of the Straits of Hormuz. This war should also increase demand for defense and engineering services. The investment case is further strengthened by KBR’s focus on improving margin quality by increasing exposure to higher-margin, technology-driven, and recurring revenue streams. With a healthy backlog, an improving margin profile, and valuation at a significant discount versus historical levels, the risk-reward appears favorable.

The balance sheet is also strong, with 2.2x net debt to EBITDA that offers further upside from accretive M&As. I believe at the current levels, Plaquemines rolling off and Lake Charles not going forward, along with nearer-term disruption in the Middle East, are already getting priced in. The war, while unfortunate and a near-term headwind, should eventually benefit both its segments, with Mission Technology benefiting from defense-related spending and STS benefiting from energy independence and repair and rebuilding of energy infrastructure work. The Lake Charles LNG also has better chances of finding a financial backer post the recent Straits of Hormuz closure than before. Hence, I believe the stock can see some partial re-rating and narrow the gap with its historical valuation levels. So, I have a buy rating on it.

Risks

A prolonged war in the Middle East may continue to pressure projects there due to war-related disruption.

In addition to organic growth, the company also relies on bolt-on M&As. Inorganic growth carries risks related to overpaying for an acquisition, not realizing intended synergies, and other integration-related challenges.

Conclusion

While KBR faces some near-term headwinds from Middle East disruptions, the Plaquemines LNG project roll-off, and the Lake Charles project delay, they are already getting priced in with the stock trading at a 40% discount versus historical P/E. The increased emphasis on energy security and independence, healthy order trends in STS business, repair work related to energy infrastructure, and defense-related logistics work make the company's prospects attractive in the medium to long run. The company is also taking the right steps, like focusing on higher-margin work and increasing exposure to Opex-related work. This positions it well in the long run. I find risk-reward attractive and hence rate the stock a Buy.

data.title: FELG: A Price Correction Makes It An Attractive Growth ETF For Dip Buyers And Long-Term Investors
data.description:
Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF’s (FELG) price correction offers an attractive buying opportunity, as its valuations plunged to the multiyear low. Moreover, FELG’s portfolio of 112 large-cap growth stocks, with half of the concentration in tech stocks and exposure to the Magnificent 7 group, boosts its potential to deliver solid returns during a recovery phase and over the long term. Furthermore, the fund’s low per-share price makes it a worth considering ETF for retail investors. Its expense ratio of 0.18% is low, and liquidity is healthy. Therefore, I initiate coverage of FELG with a buy rating.

FELG Appears Cheap After a Price Correction

Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF currently trades around $36 per share, down 13% from its recent peak of $43 per share. Technically, securities are deemed to be in a correction phase after declining more than 10% from their 52-week high. A double-digit price decline along with a strong earnings outlook has helped in cooling large-cap growth valuations to the multiyear low. The category currently trades around 22x its forward earnings, down from its recent peak of around 30x. Its forward P/E is currently trading at its lowest level since late 2023.

A significant decline in FELG’s forward P/E is attributed to a steep decline in the tech sector valuations. The information technology sector currently trades around its lowest level since the bear market of 2022. The sector makes up half of FELG’s portfolio weight. In addition, the mega-7 valuations also eased to 23x compared to their recent peak of 32x.

War-Driven Selloff and FELG’s Price Upside

The latest downtrend in FELG’s share price is provoked by the Middle East war. History tells us that the war-driven selloffs are often temporary, with the stock market bouncing back to new highs in the short to midterm. For example, the S&P 500 plunged around 7% after Russia invaded Ukraine in 2022. However, in the next few weeks, the index recovered losses despite higher oil prices. The average war-driven decline since World War II is 6%. The current selloff has exceeded the average decline.

In addition, the US President and Foreign Minister repeatedly announced that they are not interested in prolonging the war for years. They hinted at winding up military operations in the coming weeks. Therefore, it appears that the stock market is close to reaching a bottom in the current downtrend.

Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF’s share price rallied 43% since its inception in late 2023, outperforming the Russell 1000 Growth Index by 2%. Like 2026, the FELG’s price declined sharply in early 2025 due to concerns over the trade war between the largest economies. Nevertheless, the ETF recovered those losses in the next two months. Moreover, FELG’s share price rallied to a new peak in the second half, ending the year with an annual increase of 18%. I believe FELG has limited downside left, with the greater prospects of a solid rebound in the months ahead.

Earnings Growth Boosts FELG’s Share Price Upside Potential

The high-beta Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF seems to be an attractive investment vehicle for the dip buyers because of its potential to rebound sharply during a recovery phase and in the long term. The fund’s upside is likely to be supported by its robust earnings growth power.

FELG’s portfolio comprises 112 stocks. The fund seeks to outperform the Russell 1000 index using a quantitative investment approach. Its multifactor statistical models enable it to select stocks with strong fundamentals and reasonable valuations. FELG uses both top-down and bottom-up indicators to align its portfolio based on the market conditions.

With this strategy, FELG’s portfolio is spread across several growth sectors. The information technology sector accounts for half of its portfolio weight. Its significant exposure to the technology sector is among the biggest catalysts for its price upside and earnings growth. According to the FactSet data, the technology sector is expected to produce 36% year-over-year earnings growth in fiscal 2026.

Most of FELG’s top 10 holdings, representing 60% of its portfolio weight, belong to the mega-7 group. In 2026, the group is poised to accelerate its earnings growth trend by more than 20% compared to the previous year. AI is likely to be a key driver of earnings growth for the tech sector and mega caps. In addition, the tech giants massive capital investments in the AI space are likely to fuel their future earnings growth and investor confidence.

For example, Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) recently surprised investors by presenting a capital spending plan in the range of $175 to $185 billion. The company had also crossed $400 billion in annual revenue in 2025, up 15% from the year-ago period. Its net income soared 32% year over year.

Similarly, AI-powered chip makers such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), are expected to deliver 73% and 63% earnings growth in 2026. Microsoft is forecasted to produce 22% earnings growth in 2026, as its intelligent cloud business is growing at a robust pace. Here is what Satya Nadella, chairman and chief executive officer of Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), stated about AI:

We are only at the beginning phases of AI diffusion and already Microsoft has built an AI business that is larger than some of our biggest franchises. We are pushing the frontier across our entire AI stack to drive new value for our customers and partners.

On the other hand, FELG’s 20% portfolio tilt towards the financials, industrials, and healthcare stocks increases the diversification factor. These three sectors are also poised to deliver a high single- to double-digit earnings growth in 2026. According to the FELG’s data, its portfolio is forecasted to deliver 25% earnings growth in 2026, which I believe is high enough to drive a solid share price upside. The S&P 500’s forecasted earnings growth for 2026 is 15%.

Quant Ratings and Other Characteristics

In 2025, the Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF mostly traded in a buy range based on the Seeking Alpha quant analysis. However, in 2026, FELG's quant score declined due to its share price selloff. A low momentum score and higher risk factor negatively impacted its overall ratings. I believe its momentum score will improve once the war-driven volatility fades. Moreover, dividends are less relevant when it comes to generating profits through a faster price upside. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.18% is lower than the median of all ETFs. In addition, its average share trading volume of 800K is higher than the median of all ETFs of 41 thousand.

The fund’s one of the most interesting characteristics is its low per-share price of $36. A low per-share price boosts retail and new investors ability to buy shares in a large quantity. A large number of popular growth ETFs trade at significantly higher prices due to the robust bull run in the past few years.

Risk Factors

As the Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF offers a targeted exposure to growth portfolios with a significant concentration in tech stocks, its price can fall faster than the broader large-cap indices. Year to date, FELG’s price declined more than 13% compared to the S&P 500’s selloff of 6%. The fund can also respond to the trade tariffs, economic conditions, and monetary policies. Its standard deviation and annualized price volatility of 16 and 22% are higher than the median of all ETFs of 10 and 16%, respectively.

In Conclusion

The fear over Middle East war has created attractive buying opportunities for dip buyers and long-term investors. Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Growth ETF is one of the best investment vehicles to capitalize on the potential recovery and long-term returns. FELG’s valuations are currently hovering around the multiyear low, while the robust earnings growth power boosts its potential to create a solid uptrend. The fund’s expense ratio is lower than the median of all ETFs, while liquidity is healthy to create an ease in the trading process.

data.title: RDVI Vs. JEPI: Why I'm Selling The Income King For This 8.5% Yield Alternative
data.description:
Investment Thesis

For each of my articles, readers often leave numerous comments, to which I always try to respond, as it allows me to learn a great deal. While some readers ask interesting questions, other readers share their strategies and portfolios, and still others add new insights to my work, providing more accurate information and the latest news to clarify what I’ve previously written. For this, thank you all, since this is one of the main ways I improve as an analyst. I can't even imagine how much I wouldn't know if I didn't have quality feedback from readers.

A while back, one of my posts received an interesting comment recommending the FT Vest Rising Dividend Achievers Target Inc ETF (RDVI), an improved version of the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI) that offers a yield-focused strategy for a low-volatility market. Without a doubt, the market is going through a difficult period right now, and instead of low market volatility, the VIX is rising above 30. Despite this, RDVI’s performance has held up well, with its share price movements roughly tracking those of its benchmarks—the State Street SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY) and the Invesco QQQ Trust, Series 1 ETF (QQQ). That makes me think, if nothing else, about how RDVI managers are able to adapt to a chaotic market, mostly because they're betting on dividend aristocrats.

Even though both funds are designed to generate high income, their strategies are different: while JEPI is a kind of alternative to a 60/40 portfolio with an emphasis on defensive stocks, RDVI focuses on capitalizing on quality growth by investing in a basket of dividend aristocrats. Under the current conditions of geopolitical instability and increased risks of the Fed shifting its monetary policy toward higher interest rates, RDVI's strategy appears preferable because, in contrast to JEPI, its strategy combines the fundamental strength of growth stocks with a structure of ATM options on the index. Thanks to this, RDVI provides higher total returns and better NAV stability, providing investors with a balance between payouts and risks.

Prior to this article, I had examined JEPI, first comparing it with Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) and then with NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). Both times, my preference leaned toward its competitors, and I assigned JEPI a "Hold" rating. Looking at this fund through the lens of RDVI today, it's like I've found a new perfect alternative that I'm ready to recommend as a "Buy," but I'm lowering my rating on JEPI to "Sell" since there's this issue of capital erosion. I was intrigued by RDVI’s investment strategy, focusing on the broader market while selecting companies demonstrating high-quality dividend growth. This makes RDVI somewhat reminiscent of the Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), which added nearly 14% in capital appreciation to a dividend yield of 3.30% over the past year.

Fundamental Similarities Between RDVI And JEPI

Although the purpose of this article is to highlight the differences between RDVI and JEPI and demonstrate the superiority of the former, let us first note their common similarities.

  • Both funds employ a hybrid strategy that combines equity holdings with the use of options to generate additional cash flow.
  • Its goal is to provide investors with the opportunity to earn returns that exceed dividend yields.
  • It selects large-cap companies as its asset class.
  • Both funds make monthly payments with the same yield as part of their dividend policy.

Fundamental Differences Between RDVI And JEPI

More in-depth analysis of ETFs helps identify the key differences between them. This main criterion relates to their investment strategy, including stock selection, the income generation mechanism, and management style. Although RDVI is more unique, they still use a strict index-based approach, choosing stocks of companies that demonstrate dividend growth as their assets. The important factor for ETF managers is not only how much dividends the selected assets pay, however, but also how actively they increase them while maintaining a healthy balance sheet and high financial stability. RDVI's portfolio consists of 74 holdings, of which the top 10 account for 25.85%.

As an income-generating mechanism, it uses a Target Income strategy based on selling call options (usually FLEX). RDVI uses an "at-the-money" options strategy against the S&P 500 rather than selling options on individual stocks to reach a target return level that exceeds stock dividends. Within this strategy, an exercise price is selected that is relatively close to the asset’s current market price, thus limiting upside potential. However, partial coverage is used, since RDVI generally sells options for an amount equivalent to approximately 20% of NAV. With this approach, fund managers allow the portfolio to capture the remaining approximately 80% of the gains from rising stock prices.

Portfolio managers at JEPI employ a more active strategy, picking stocks with low volatility and greater fundamental undervaluation. The advantage of JEPI may prove to be that its portfolio has a low correlation with the technology sector. The share of Information Technology stocks accounts for only 14.4% of the total portfolio, whereas RDVI’s is 23.91%. JEPI’s portfolio itself consists of 120 holdings, of which the top 10 account for only 15.75%. The fund’s portfolio is thus more diversified.

In order to generate income, a strategy is used whereby, rather than selling covered call options on the S&P 500, contracts for ELNs are purchased from a counterparty bank. These instruments are unique in that they mimic the returns from selling call options, but this comes at the cost of additional risk related to the counterparty’s solvency. But I don’t think this is a problem, because JP Morgan Chase & Co. (JPM) acts as the counterparty.

Although the dividend yields of the two funds are virtually identical, RDVI has the advantage of offering stable payouts that have historically ranged between $0.15 and $0.19 (with a moderate upward trend). Dividends from JEPI are not as stable and have shown an annual decline. As an example, payments in 2022 reached $0.61–0.62, compared to $0.35 in recent years. This can be explained by the fact that RDVI’s option coverage ratio is around 20%, meaning that the volatility of its premium from the option strategy will be significantly lower.

Because of this, though, RDVI has a better chance of delivering faster growth in total returns than JEPI, as the "growth ceiling" issue is less of a concern for the former. Furthermore, RDVI's portfolio is specifically selected from companies with growing dividends, which are less volatile and have more stable market value growth. JEPI is in a less favorable position because the way it uses ELNs makes it impossible to capture the full market growth. In particular, this is the case if volatility exceeds the beta. Then again, that could help the fund be more defensive during a bear market, even though I haven’t seen that happen in 2026. In the end, for the 3-year period, the Total Return RDVI was 25.98% higher.

The table below provides a more detailed comparison of RDVI and JEPI.

The Problem Of JEPI Erosion And The Advantages Of RDVI

One of the main reasons why RDVI has an advantage over JEPI lies in the latter fund’s mechanics, leading to capital erosion. When compared to funds that use leverage, JEPI’s erosion of NAV is not self-destructive but rather due to the specific nature of its option strategy through ELN. Firstly, the managers of JEPI sell call options on the S&P 500 (in the ELN structure), limiting the potential for growth. In a strong bull market, the short options positions cut off the top of the gains from the rally.

Secondly, JEPI's dividend yield is not correlated with total returns from stock price appreciation and option premiums, because this would not lead to an inevitable decline in NAV. The desire to pay a high dividend yield most likely exacerbates the problem of capital erosion during periods of low market volatility, since this reduces the option premium.

Finally, while JEPI selects low-volatility core stocks for its portfolio, they tend to ignore fast-growing stocks in certain sectors, and this has caused the core portfolio to underperform the broad index. When evaluating RDVI, we see that its portfolio has greater resilience to capital erosion, as reflected in the strategy's higher performance. Portfolio managers at RDVI select stronger dividend-paying companies that have demonstrated better growth and are more resilient to market downturns. Furthermore, RDVI uses options directly as part of its strategy, thereby reducing hidden fees that impact NAV over the long term.

Risks To The Investment Thesis

There are some risks associated with investing in RDVI, which can be specific to the fund’s hybrid structure. In my view, there are two main types of risks: market risks and structural risks. The former, I believe, represent the most significant risks. This is because the current market turmoil is driving up risk-free rates (US Treasury yields), and this, in turn, reduces the attractiveness of dividend-paying stocks. Therefore, investors are shifting into bonds, taking capital out of assets like RDVI.

The main risk among structural risks is the limitation of growth potential. Even though RDVI’s historical returns have been significantly higher than those of JEPI, it still uses a mechanism whereby part of the future growth of selected stocks is sold off to generate the target income. That’s why, when the market is bullish, RDVI’s performance will lag behind that of the S&P 500. To illustrate, comparing RDVI not to JEPI but to SPY over a 3-year period reveals the opposite: Total Return lags by 9.88% instead of a 25.98% advantage. Nevertheless, given the choice between receiving approximately 8.5% in dividend yield through monthly payments or settling for capital gains, most would likely choose the former.

Conclusion

Concluding this article, my takeaway is that despite JEPI’s larger portfolio and AUM, this fund’s overall returns are significantly lower compared to RDVI and the S&P 500 benchmarks. For this reason, my recommendation for this fund has been downgraded to "Sell," because I do not see this instrument as attractive in 2026. In contrast, RDVI demonstrates a high-quality evolution of its yield-generating strategy, delivering 8.5% annually. This fund's advantage lies in its combination of dividend aristocrats and partial option hedging of about 20% of NAV. These factors allow for a golden balance between high dividend yields and capital preservation, minimizing the problem of a growth ceiling. Without a doubt, this phase of the market is characterized by high chaos and volatility, and the key risk is rising interest rates. But adding RDVI to your portfolio provides a reliable income-generating asset that, in spite of the chaos, is able to generate solid returns.

data.title: Nexstar Stuck In Limbo As Judge Halts Tegna Integration
data.description:
Nexstar Media Group, Inc. (NXST) is the largest broadcast TV operator in the United States. And it appeared set to become significantly larger with its pending acquisition of rival Tegna, which until recently traded under the ticker symbol "TGNA."

I last covered Nexstar stock in November. In that piece, I downgraded shares to a hold, arguing that the risk/reward picture was not favorable due to the merger-related uncertainty. The market didn't seem to worry much about said risks, however, bidding NXST stock up to new highs to start 2026. However, the merger has now come into question, and the stock has given back all the recent gains:

So, it's a great time to update the investment case for Nexstar. Why did the stock plunge over the past week, and is there an opportunity at this juncture?

The Deal Closed, But The Legal Challenges Haven't

Much of the market's enthusiasm for NXST stock came on the theory that the Tegna acquisition was successfully clearing regulatory hurdles and on pace for a rapid close. That was true, to an extent.

The Federal Communications Commission ("FCC") gave the deal its go-ahead earlier in March. Between that and getting approval from the Department of Justice ("DOJ"), there was considerable momentum in favor of the tie-up. Using those approvals, Nexstar officially closed the deal on March 19th, with Nexstar CEO Perry Sook commenting:

“This transaction is essential to sustaining strong local journalism in the communities we serve. By bringing these two outstanding companies together, Nexstar will be a stronger, more dynamic enterprise—better positioned to deliver exceptional journalism and local programming with enhanced assets, capabilities, and talent. We are grateful to President Trump, Chairman Carr, and the DOJ for recognizing the dynamic forces shaping the media landscape and enabling this transaction to move forward.”

Sook expressed his thanks to President Trump and others in the U.S. government that used their influence to help advance the transaction. But those aren't necessarily the only people who have a say in whether the deal should have gone ahead or not.

Those lingering concerns came to the forefront this week, when Judge Troy Nunley of the Eastern District of California issued a 24-page restraining order. Nunley's order blocks Nexstar from integrating Tegna into its operations. Rather, Nexstar and Tegna will have to continue operating as two distinct businesses for the time being. Management will remain separate, and the order blocks the sharing of confidential business information between the two firms. Nunley found that this was necessary because the deal "is presumed likely to ‌violate ⁠antitrust laws based on the combined firm market share alone."

There are now eight states that have filed in opposition to the merger, along with competitor DirecTV.

Bulls seemed to take the view that as long as President Trump and federal government regulators were in favor of the deal, it would ultimately be able to go ahead. However, there remains significant legal complexity. For example, the FCC long maintained a cap on how much market concentration any one TV station ownership group could have. The Nexstar/Tegna entity would reach 80% of American households, far above the 39% regulatory limit.

There is uncertainty over whether the FCC had the authority to waive this cap specifically for the Nexstar/Tegna deal or if that is something that would need Congressional authorization. I'm not a legal expert, but my understanding is that there is a good chance that legislators, not the FCC, have authority here. And there is likely to be significant pushback, especially in the event that Democrats regain control of Congress in the November 2026 midterm elections. To the extent that the Nexstar/Tegna deal approval could seem like a partisan effort that was pushed through quickly while Republicans were in power, there is a risk that the pendulum swings against the deal if Democrats fare better in the next election. To that point, various senators are now raising further questions about the deal approval process.

There is plenty of legal back-and-forth still ahead of this deal, despite it having closed and Nexstar now owning Tegna's assets. I don't have a strong opinion on how the legal arguments will play out. That said, risk is still clearly elevated until there is more clarity on whether Nexstar actually gets to integrate the Tegna assets or not.

Nexstar's Forward Outlook

The main bullish appeal for Nexstar is the attractive valuation. In fact, a quick glance at the firm's earnings estimates makes Nexstar look like quite a value following the recent pullback:

There are a few caveats to consider, however, before concluding that NXST stock is at 7x earnings and thus a Strong Buy.

First, there is a cycle here due to political advertising. Earnings and revenue should be up sharply in 2026 with heavy political ad spending tied to midterm elections. Without that, in 2027, earnings will probably fall sharply. Analysts see an even greater surge in 2028, as Nexstar will benefit from both congressional races along with ads tied to the next U.S. presidential election. In 2029, without the election catalyst, EPS should drop back to near 2027 levels.

Furthermore, these are adjusted earnings, and Nexstar often runs into other expenses. In 2025, on a GAAP basis, the company was barely profitable due to expenses tied to the Tegna merger along with amortization of intangible assets. It's safe to assume that legal costs will remain elevated as long as the Tegna situation remains stuck in the courts. And, as long as Nexstar keeps consolidating the TV industry, it will generate additional goodwill and intangible assets that later become accounting costs. For companies engaged in perpetual M&A, there can be a significant drag between reported adjusted earnings and the actual amount of cash available to return to shareholders.

Furthermore, Nexstar is taking on a lot more debt to fund the Tegna deal. If the integration is completed in a reasonable amount of time, Nexstar can start obtaining its budgeted merger synergies and achieve anticipated EPS growth out of the acquisition. But, in a scenario where Tegna remains an independent operation due to drawn-out legal squabbles, there could be a significant downside to Nexstar's earnings as the added interest expense weighs things down.

In the bigger picture, I am also turning a bit more cautious on Nexstar's advertising outlook. The economy appears to be potentially losing steam, with the U.S. labor market showing increasing strain. That's likely to be reflected in a weaker advertising market, especially if oil prices remain elevated. The market had also baked in a Fed rate cut cycle, which was supposed to help businesses such as car dealerships that advertise on TV. That potential economic tailwind is now in jeopardy.

And, on the political ad part of the business, bulls seem to view Nexstar as having an inherent right to a growing revenue stream going forward. However, my understanding is that relatively few people under the age of around 40 now watch broadcast TV, at least for anything other than live sports. As such, politicians that rely primarily on TV ads will be missing an increasingly large chunk of the overall voting demographic. I'm not saying TV political ads are going away, but I do think there will be an increasingly rapid shift within the overall political advertising pie toward digital channels.

NXST Stock Verdict

When I last covered Nexstar, the stock was at $184, and we were still much earlier along in the merger process. Today, shares are around the same price.

On the one hand, you could argue that Nexstar is in a better position since they already got the deal through the first wave of regulatory blocks and, in fact, have already acquired Tegna. It's probably harder for judges or politicians to put the genie back in the bottle now that Tegna has already been purchased.

However, Nexstar arguably now faces significantly more downside risk than before. They are stuck with the financing and associated interest expenses tied to the Tegna deal with no guarantee of a commensurate payoff. Nexstar was budgeting $300 million in cost savings from the combination. Now, it has to pay interest on the acquisition but isn't going to be able to tap into these anticipated cost synergies until the legal battles end. And it's conceivable that judges or Congress will ultimately block this entirely, forcing Nexstar to divest Tegna. It's hard to guess what valuation Nexstar would get for Tegna in that sort of asset sale scenario.

And, putting the merger to the side, I'd argue that Nexstar's economic outlook has worsened since my coverage last fall. The surge in oil prices, in particular, could pressure consumer spending and thus have significant negative repercussions for local businesses that advertise on Nexstar-operated stations.

I'm not bearish enough to downgrade to a Sell rating given the recent sharp retreat in Nexstar's stock price. That said, I have no interest in buying NXST shares on this recent correction, either.

data.title: Games Workshop: IP Engine Still Fully Not Online
data.description:
Games Workshop Group PLC (GMWKF) is managing solid performance improvements on the basis of stronger sales volumes and, no doubt, pricing improvements, though the exact breakdown isn't being disclosed. Investments continue to be made to scale the logistics and manufacturing as the hobby grows in popularity, and the market is apparently tolerating another 4% price increase started in October 2025. The only thing that is a little bit of a concern is the retail growth is not that great, which may be signaling some changing dynamics of their markets where the tabletop game is taking a backseat to the interest in models and painting and the lore. We think this does point to the importance of the licensing engine, which is not really online yet and down YoY after the pretty successful Space Marine 2 release, which we discussed in our previous coverage.

Results

Volumes have to have been pretty good. Gross margins grew considerably by almost 2% owed to higher volumes and therefore greater efficiency despite some negative tariff effects.

Further warehouse efficiencies are expected in 2027 when another facility opens. Additionally, Factory 4 investments have been made in this half-year, and they should scale manufacturing further to cope with what seems to be secularly growing demand for the hobby.

In terms of the core revenue growth, there were 12 new stores opened, a little over 3% growth in the retail footprint. But the overall retail growth as part of the core revenue was only 5.4%. Trade drove growth at 25.2%. The retail channel is where they actually host the games and do the player onboarding to the tabletop. It's a pretty essential part of the actual gaming experience of Warhammer. Slower growth here but still strong sales of minis tell us that maybe the interest in the hobby is broadening beyond the gaming component. That would also imply that the draw of Warhammer's lore is going to have to become more important. It's also a bit of a concern because one of the few gatekeepers preventing the hobby from eventually being dominated by 3D-printed minis, particularly with 3D printing costs going down and mini costs going up, is the fact that many of the games played with the minis (specifically tournament events, which are common) are often under the watchful eye of store managers.

While GW is very conservative with their IP, which limits some of the IP handling risks, the concern then simply is whether or not the company is doing enough to monetize it. While Space Marine 2 made for a little bit of a tough comp, the big contributions are still coming down the pipe but could take some years. GW eventually came to an agreement with Amazon (AMZN), but the contribution is likely very deferred since there are still no details released whatsoever as to the creative direction of the show - like will it feature Henry Cavill as a space marine or a more grounded story in the 40k lore? We don't respect Warhammer+; we see limited value added there over what's available for free on YouTube in terms of Warhammer content. Amazon is helming the major ship for GW's IP, even compared to announcements of Dawn of War IV (DoW III was not a success) and Total War: Warhammer 40,000, which may run into trouble with Creative Assembly having lost some ground in a bad run of Total War games, particularly Total War: Pharoah.

Bottom Line

The coming price increase on average is going to be around 4% on the minis. Price increases were similar last year, so there was probably a lot of volume growth. Probably around 12% in volume growth. With price increases continuing, we have every reason to expect further margin lift. Investments in Factor 4 and incoming warehouse investments should also continue to help lift operating profits. The minis continue to sell, and the lore is likely going to become increasingly popular; it still isn't fully mainstream yet, we'd say. But the stock isn't cheap at 30x forward PE. A very close comp isn't easy to find; we'd maybe say Hasbro (HAS) at around 16x forward PE, which has other nerd properties like Wizards of the Coast (WotC) and related IPs like Dungeons and Dragons and Magic the Gathering, which get leveraged well into things like Baldur's Gate. While the manufacturing business should continue to provide profit growth, for GW to be particularly attractive, things need to really happen in licensing, which is a very high incremental profit business. We think it's possible to play the stock around news flow from gaming and media journalism to get a read on when the Amazon show might be coming and how good it might be and look to invest closer to the release of that show and the addition of new people to the hobby as a catalyst in addition to the explicit licensing sales. Also, we are a little concerned about the 3D printing risk. It's an impairment concern for us, particularly as the minis become really quite unaffordable in our view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Market Is Giving You A Gift, Don't Overthink It
data.description:
It has been a little over a month since I wrote the article “Not A Bear Market Yet, But It’s Already A Stock Picker’s Dream.” My opinion on the market in general was simple: the main indexes had already undergone a certain correction, but the real opportunity was in stock picking.

Now, I think the scenario has already changed. I am still a stock picker, and I focus on that. But the market in general is very undervalued. Looking at indexes like the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, you are buying excellent companies at valuations that are already very interesting.

I don't think it's something as generalized as “buy anything and it will go up,” but on average, speaking of indexes and good ETFs, I am already much more optimistic, and the scenario seems to me to be the type of case that is rare to find.

S&P 500 And Nasdaq: Quality Companies With Reasonable Valuation

Since I am a stock picker and a fundamental analyst, I will start by talking about what you are buying when you buy US Indexes.

Everyone knows that when you buy an S&P 500 (SP500) ETF like SPY or IVV, you are buying the 500 largest American companies.

But more specifically, you have a higher weight for gigantic companies. Not surprisingly, Nvidia (NVDA) is 7% of the entire index, Apple (AAPL) is 6%, Microsoft (MSFT) is 5%, Amazon (AMZN) is 3.5%, and Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) is a little over 5%, adding the two stocks present in the index. The top 10 holdings (SPY) already account for more than ⅓ of the entire index.

This is something that at certain times can be bad, but at other times can be good. In the long run, obviously, it is an allocation and a way of rebalancing the index that was very successful and brought many gains. But at certain times it may seem like “too much” to have 7% of Nvidia or to have ~30% of the portfolio in tech.

But I don't think that is the current moment.

Tech, and especially the Mag 7, have gone through such a challenging moment in recent months that having this focus on tech and mega caps becomes interesting and asymmetric.

I still see some people who are anchored in the thinking from a few years ago that Nvidia is super overvalued and that you have to pay a premium to participate in this growth. This is no longer true.

Note below how the P/E of these companies has decreased.

Now, note how the EPS of these companies has risen.

This is what I am talking about. Of course, you still have the other ~70% composed of other companies from different sectors, where some are at a fair valuation, others overvalued, and others even cheaper than the mega caps. But in general, this is already a proxy to say that the main companies in the index are not doing poorly; they are improving, and meanwhile, multiples are compressing.

Adding these two factors, we have a better margin of safety for the main companies in the index, and as a “bonus,” you still get good diversification in case the market is right about hyperscalers being a riskier choice now, you possess commodities in case there is a commodity supercycle, and so on.

By the way, bringing an anecdote to the article, I don't remember the last time I felt this way. I am cautious in my purchases and new additions to the portfolio, with a lot of patience. Now I feel that there are so many opportunities at once that it is even difficult to avoid "over diversifying" the portfolio. Microsoft, for instance, is a company that I still don't have in my portfolio, but at this price I want to have it and start a position; recently I did this with Meta (META), and I am also increasing my position in Amazon. So there are so many attractive options that it seems difficult even to choose (and this is something an ETF could help with).

Why This Is The Best Time To Buy

Moving to more technical and momentum factors, to prove that this is a rare momentum, note in the chart below how both QQQ and SPY have very rarely been below their exponential moving average (200) in the last 10 years.

For both the Nasdaq and the S&P 500, the last time was during the sharp correction at the beginning of 2025 and also in the bear market of 2022 if we disregard other dips that were fast and corrected in a short time.

For me, combining the EMA (200) with the qualitative part of the S&P 500 is already enough to defend a buy. Buying quality companies, arguably the best in the world, below a historical average is important.

Besides that, the P/E is another important point. It is not something exclusive to mega caps. Even taking a long history, such as 10 years, the QQQ P/E is well below the average. 21.5x for the next 12 months against an average of 24.5x. For the S&P 500, the average is 19.7x, and the P/E is already at 19.8x. And this includes some outliers, like the dip in 2019, the pandemic crash, etc.

A Word About Cycles And Time Arbitrage

But of course, all this that I mentioned does not guarantee that the investor will have returns in the short term. Even being below the 200-day average, even having an attractive P/E, and even having many quality companies and compounders, the next quarters could be even worse.

Let's assume that, for whatever reason, AI is something that will not yield results. Data centers and electricity become expensive to the point of making AI agents unfeasible, or AI agents bring neither a revenue increase nor a margin increase for the companies that are investing in this. Truly the index would deserve to fall and go through a worse bear market than what we are seeing. This is because this affects not only hyperscalers like Microsoft, Amazon, and Alphabet, but it also affects the expectations of several companies in the index.

And if the estimates are wrong, then the forward P/E is also wrong. Think, for example, that if Alphabet's net income is 10% lower in a 20-year flow, that is more or less the correction Alphabet has to have. But in fact, the level of correction could be even worse, because it is not just shareholder value that matters to the market; there is also the issue of the discount rate and safety. Perhaps the market still sees a total return of ~10% for the main tech stocks but doesn't think it is “enough” for the uncertainties and then prefers to have a total return of ~5% in Walmart (WMT) or Costco (COST) (rough assumptions just to prove my point).

These are uncertainties that deserve to be considered. But I am not a short-term trader, and therefore, my arguments here are not for a short-term trade. For this reason, I think it's worth talking a little about cycles and time arbitrage.

Recently I wrote an entire article about time arbitrage and how it can be a “superpower.” I highly recommend that you read it here. But in summary, I believe it is possible to have an edge by investing in a timeframe different from the market.
