Even if the market is right about the fear of AI, CapEx, and the like, and in the next 2 years the free cash flow is lower than the consensus, many of the cases that delivered a poor performance are still intact in the long term.

Microsoft, for example, even if Azure does not grow 40% for a long time, still has a global infrastructure business that is a compounder (at a pace close to double digits), and this comes with tons of optionalities that could materialize in the next 10 years. This is also true for Amazon, Alphabet, and several others in the index.

So buying the S&P 500, which is full of the best companies in the world at a reasonable valuation with a “good enough” expected growth, is already more than enough to have good returns, as long as you wait long enough and are willing to endure the volatility (and that is where factors like asset allocation and having fixed income, among others, come in).

Besides that, I think it's important to talk about cyclicality. In recent years we saw a cycle very favorable to the US and specifically to tech stocks. Consequently, I see many people saying that now is the time to invest in international stocks, emerging markets, etc.

I don't totally disagree with this view. There are opportunities in emerging markets. And being Brazilian, I know that if there is a commodity supercycle, the Brazilian index will do very well (and possibly will outperform the S&P 500). This is because it is still a stock market and a domestic economy with significant exposure to oil, iron ore, agricultural commodities, etc.

But going back to the subject of US equities, I think many let something go unnoticed. In the 2000s, if Brazil did very well, that mattered little to the main US companies. Maybe one or two would benefit together, etc.

Nowadays it is not like that. Microsoft is a global company, Alphabet is a global company, and Meta is a global company. All these “tech stocks,” in fact, have exposure to most world economies. If Latin America delivers an economic boom in the coming years, that means a population more willing to spend, companies with more demand for ads, and ads that perform better, and this also affects tech stocks in a certain way.

Illustrating this, Netflix (NFLX) had $5.3 billion in revenue in the UCAN region (US and Canada) in Q4. But in Latin America (LATAM), the revenue is $1.4 billion. Asia Pacific (APAC) is also $1.4 billion. Joining these emerging markets, we have an extremely important revenue line impacted by currency and by domestic economies, which, in a good cycle for emerging markets, also affects Netflix. Of course, I am not saying that these two revenues will be able to “save” the stock in an American recession. What I am saying is that if US revenue growth stops growing at 18% and starts growing at 8%, but APAC and LATAM continue growing at ~20% (or even accelerate), Netflix remains a growth company.

In other words, even though it is heavily tech-focused, the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq not only remain quite solid; in fact, they are even stronger now, with companies with a moat wider than 20 years ago.

Bottom Line

In short, I think in a few weeks we went from “we have to be cautious in selecting stocks” to “the US market is appealing” in general. As I mentioned throughout the article, I don't think the momentum is risk-free; there truly are uncertainties. But looking at it as a long-term investor, the US market offers quality compounders for a reasonable (or cheap) valuation.

data.title: 3 Market Predictions For April
data.description:
Here we are on the last day of March as the first quarter comes to a merciful end for investors, albeit a nice rally took place Tuesday morning. We are coming to the close of the quarter with both the Dow and NASDAQ in official correction territory, while the S&P 500 is off roughly 9% from its recent highs as of market close Monday. Not surprisingly, the energy sector was the only one of the 11 sectors in the S&P that posted a gain in March.

Brent oil had a record surge for a month in March, and the WTI oil benchmark crossed the $100/barrel threshold for the first time since 2022. So, what is ahead for investors in the first month of the second quarter? Three market predictions for April follow below.

Extreme Oil Volatility

Obviously, this appears to be an easy call. Brent crude moved above $110/barrel late this month for the first time since 2022. Oil's direction seems very binary right now. Brent will either end April at $135/barrel or above and push global economies to the brink of recession or will fall under $85/barrel, and global investors will breathe a huge sigh of relief.

And it all depends on which scenario plays out in the Middle East and the Strait of Hormuz. Obviously, other key commodities and energy products are highly dependent on this situation in April. These include jet fuel, diesel, gasoline, and LNG on the energy side and key ingredients for fertilizer as well as aluminum, helium, and a variety of other key compounds.

If the Strait of Hormuz is forced open this month either by a resolution to this conflict or by military force, oil should plunge back to the $85/barrel level or so on Brent. That would be above the mid- to high-$70s it was trading at prior to this regional war but still far below current levels. The equity market should see a significant bounce if things play out this week in April. Whether that rebound is sustainable given other key issues right now is less certain.

It should be noted that even if the Strait of Hormuz opened this week, it is going to take time to recover from this massive disruption. Smelters, refineries, and chemical plants take time to restart. Global supply chains will take many weeks or months to recover. Oil reserves that have been released in the U.S. and Europe will need to be rebuilt over time. And global economies will still see an uptick in inflation due to higher food, commodity, and chemical costs working their way through the logistical ecosystem. This could be offset somewhat by demand destruction. That said, economic disaster would have been averted if this global chokepoint reopened for business.

If at this time next month, the Strait of Hormuz is still effectively closed, it is hard to see how oil is not at least $135/barrel or higher. Others see oil moving to $150 to $200 a barrel in this scenario. Asia will be the first to face significant fuel shortages. China gets 35% of its oil supplies from this passage, including 15% from Iran. That said, the country does have 4 months of oil reserves in storage. Other countries like India, Japan, and Australia are not in as strong a position. Europe will see fuel shortages soon after Asia.

Higher gasoline, jet fuel, diesel, and fertilizer prices will have significant impacts on the U.S. economy and perhaps tilt it into recession, depending on how long this conflict lasts. Gas prices at the pump topped four bucks a gallon for the first time since August 2022 this week. It should be noted that the U.S. only gets 7% of its oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz. And Venezuela is now a bigger potential supplier to the U.S. given recent events in that country.

The Private Credit Pandora's Box Will Not Close In April

If it weren't for events in Iran, the fast deterioration in the private credit markets would be front and center as far as investors' key concerns around the market. Problems at Blue Owl Capital (OWL) in late February reignited fears around the private credit markets, which have seen huge growth since the Great Financial Crisis.

The Pandora's Box that is private credit got opened significantly wider in March. In the second week of the month, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) reduced the value of some loans ‌to private credit funds. This happened after the bank reviewed the impact of market turmoil around software companies, which make up a good chunk of private credits' loan book. This sent some shockwaves across the space. And growing fears of private credit contagion was one of my three predictions for March, it should be noted.

During March, there was a cacophony of large private credit funds at major players in the space that 'gated' quarterly redemption requests. These names included some of the largest funds in the industry, including BlackRock (BLK), Apollo Global Management (APO), Cliffwater, Morgan Stanley (MS), and Ares Management Corporation (ARES). Generally, investors got roughly half of the funds requested for the quarter.

Redemption requests were already moving up significantly in recent quarters at major funds. Given this quarter's gating, redemption requests in the coming quarter are likely heading significantly higher. The horses seem like they have escaped from the barn, and investors should expect more negative headlines around private credit in April. The shares of alternative investment managers are already deep in bear market territory and feel more like 'value traps' than buy-the-dip opportunities.

Gold Will Rebound

Speaking of bear market territory, gold briefly went there in March. There are several reasons the yellow metal headed down in March. The precious metal had a huge run over 2023-2025 and kind of went parabolic early this year along with silver.

In addition, the spike in interest rates during March provided a headwind for precious metals as well. This helped the dollar to post its strongest month since 2022, which helped push gold down as well during the month. There were also some significant gold sales from central banks during the month, including 60 tons from Turkey as it defends the Turkish lira. Russia has been a significant seller of gold in 2026 as well to shore up its finances as the war in Ukraine hit its fifth year in February. Higher oil and energy prices due to the conflict in the Middle East, as well as the removal of some energy sanctions, have alleviated that need, it should be noted.

Goldman Sachs was out this week reiterating they expect gold to rise to $5,400 an ounce by the end of 2026. Wells Fargo sees gold in a range of $6,100 to $6,300 an ounce by year. BMO Capital recently raised its year-end target for gold as well. Given the continuing and growing worries around fiat currencies and unsustainable sovereign debt throughout most of the G-20, the blip down in March in gold prices feels like it could have been a 'hiccup' after a long run-up.

Gold feels like it should get a decent rebound in April after big losses in March. If the Strait of Hormuz reopens in April, I will likely add to my covered call holdings in names like Newmont Mining (NEM). Gold miners should benefit as gold starts to rally again. If oil plunges, miners will doubly benefit, given energy prices make up a good chunk of their operational costs.

And those are some key ways I see the markets playing out in April. Investors faced more uncertainty to start the second quarter than at any time since volatility spiked in early April of last year when reciprocal tariffs were announced. This news triggered a huge downturn in the markets over the next week and sent the S&P 500 down 18% intraday before many of these tariff policies were 'walked back'. Are we facing a similar situation as the first quarter comes to a close? We will find out together in April.

data.title: Stocks Plunged In March, But Hope Emerges In The U.S.-Iran War
data.description:
Stocks plummeted in March as the war in Iran sent oil prices (CO1:COM) soaring, but geopolitical rays of hope emerged to close the quarter. The S&P 500 ETF (SP500, SPY) fell more than 5%, nearly its worst month since September 2022. Despite a powerful 'Turnaround Tuesday' to finish the first quarter, it was red across the board, away from Energy. The Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) shed 5%, very close to the declines in mid-caps (MDY) and the Russell 2000 ETF (IWM).

Foreign stocks were hit even harder. The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU) slid 9%, with emerging markets feeling the brunt of higher Brent and WTI crude oil prices.

In the bond market, the yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury rate climbed 34 basis points to 4.30%. The positive stock-bond correlation was negative news for the iShares Core US Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG), which suffered a 2% March decline (dividends included).

Global oil obviously took off, resulting in a 53% monthly increase in the United States Oil Fund (USO)--its best month on record (even with a loss on the 31st). Oil’s gain contrasted with a massive 12% decline in gold (GLD). The yellow metal had a hot start to the year but is now down nearly 20% from its January all-time high. Finally, bitcoin (IBIT) perhaps surprisingly rallied 3% in March, all while the US Dollar ETF (UUP) added 3%.

Oil's March Jolt Stunned Stocks

Digging into the sector view, Energy (XLE) was the big winner—no surprises there. Up more than 10% for March, oil & gas equities lagged physical oil in a major way. Still, XLE’s rise looked very strong compared to the 10 other S&P 500 sector ETFs. Worst among them was Industrials (XLI), which lost 9%. Steam was let out of the AI trade to an extent, while profit-taking in the Aerospace & Defense industry stymied XLI.

Interestingly, defensive sectors failed to provide safety. Utilities (XLU) was "least bad," down 4%. But Real Estate (XLRE), Health Care (XLV), and Staples (XLP) all endured sharp March losses. And the growth trade was also weak—tech-related companies saw their shares get re-rated. The likes of Nvidia (NVDA), Meta Platforms (META), and Microsoft (MSFT) now sport P/E ratios that are heavily discounted relative to recent years.

March S&P 500 Sector ETF Performance

U.S. Stocks Stumbled in March, Energy Shares Rose

Massive March Oil & Gas Gains, Precious Metals Plummet

Turning to the economy, March began like a lion—both geopolitically and regarding the data. The February jobs report was much worse than expected, with a 92,000 reported employment decline and a notable uptick in the jobless rate. Average hourly earnings remain fine, and we’ll get a fresh slate of March labor-market reports beginning on Wednesday.

Keep in mind that the official payrolls update hits on Good Friday (a market holiday).

U.S. Labor Market Remains Weak

All eyes will be on the consumer this week and throughout April. The now-dated February Retail Sales report hits on April 1, and there’s no fooling the reality that those numbers won’t capture now much-higher gasoline prices.

On April 30, the price for a gallon of regular unleaded topped $4 for the first time since 2022, up a dollar from the close of February. The data we do have from March suggests no meaningful consumer slowdown yet.

U.S. Retail Sales Still Decent... Before the Oil Spike

Market-watchers will also center on CPI inflation data later in April. As of February, the YoY headline CPI rate was a somewhat tame 2.4%. That’s set to change meaningfully, thanks to the eye-popping surge in oil prices. U.S. inflation could rise above 3% YoY in short order, but the expectation is that WTI and RBOB gasoline will drift lower as 2026 progresses (at least according to the respective futures curves).

The good news is that the economy will begin lapping increased tariff rates on the 12-month lookback.

U.S. Inflation Rate Poised To Tick Up

U.S. Gasoline > $4/Gallon

As for the Fed, outgoing Chair Powell offered relief to markets on March’s penultimate day. In a fireside chat with Harvard University students, the Fed chief downplayed the chance of a rate hike.

Indeed, the Fed Funds futures market moved. There’s now a scant market-implied chance of a quarter-point tightening between now and year-end. Before the speech, futures priced in close to an 80% probability of a hike. Further de-escalation rhetoric between the U.S. and Iran helped squash those chances on the final trading day of the month, too.

Fed Expected to Hold Its Policy Rate

Ironically, U.S. real GDP growth expectations have been relatively stable. Prediction markets peg the inflation-adjusted Q1 pace of economic expansion at 2.6%.

That’s down a bit from a month ago, but it’s really not much damage, all told.

Still-Solid Q1 U.S. Real GDP Seen

What’s not turning worse? S&P 500 earnings per share estimates. According to FactSet, both CY 2026 and CY 2027 EPS forecasts are on the rise. Couple the drastic S&P 500 loss in March with rising profit expectations, and the market’s price-to-earnings multiple has compressed massively.

The U.S. large-cap index sported a teens P/E before the solid March 31 rally.

S&P 500 EPS Estimates on the Rise

Looking ahead, April has historically delivered solid returns. The average S&P 500 gain is 1.1% from 2010 to 2025, but the last two instances have been negative.

We’ll see if the 2025 playbook continues to unfold—it's two straight years of an up November, down December, up January, and down February & March. The bulls would certainly take that trade, given the major May-September climbs in 2024 and 2025.

S&P 500: Bullish Seasonality Ahead

The Bottom Line

Stocks plunged in March, but glimmers of hope came about on the final day of the month. Global equities suffered the largest monthly loss since September 2022 as the war in Iran caused oil prices to skyrocket. Geopolitical uncertainty remains very high heading into April, with macro eyes on the Strait of Hormuz.

Still, economists will parse key data ahead, as investors and traders monitor price action. The S&P 500 came very close to technical correction territory toward the end of March. The war, AI’s impact, private credit, and a soft labor market remain headwinds.
